1: וַיַּעַל אַבְרָם מִמִּצְרַיִם (wayyaʿal ʾaḇrām mimmitsrāyim) — "Abram went up from Egypt." ʿĀlāh (Qal waw-consecutive, to go up, ascend) — directional language that is topographically precise: Canaan is higher than the Nile Delta, so one always "goes up" from Egypt to Canaan. But in the OT the verb carries canonical weight beyond geography: going up to Canaan and going up to Jerusalem become theological movements (Ezra 1:3; Genesis 2:1; the post-exilic returns are all described with ʿālāh). Abram's return from Egypt with his wife and wealth complete (unlike the Egypt episode in Genesis 12) signals restoration of the narrative that the famine had temporarily suspended.
2: כָּבֵד מְאֹד בַּמִּקְנֶה בַּכֶּסֶף וּבַזָּהָב (kāḇēḏ mĕʾōḏ bammiqneh bakkeseḵ ûḇazzāhāḇ) — "very heavy/weighty in livestock and in silver and gold." Kāḇēḏ (heavy, weighty) is the standard word for being wealthy or laden — the same root as kāḇôḏ (glory, honor, weightiness). The semantic field connects material wealth and social honor: to be heavy with goods is to be "glorious" in the eyes of the ancient world. The enumeration of livestock, silver, and gold will recur in the patriarchal wealth-formulas (Genesis 24:35; Genesis 26:14) — the MKT reflects the material texture of ANE pastoral economics.
3: לְמַסָּעָיו (lĕmasāʿāyw, by his stages/journeys) — from nāsaʿ (to pull up tent stakes and set out; to journey by stages). The noun massāʿ (journey stage, stopping place) appears most densely in Numbers 33, where Israel's wilderness journey is recorded as a list of massāʾôt (stopping-places). Abram's return to Bethel stage by stage echoes the wilderness wandering — the patriarchal sojourn and the Sinai sojourn are structurally parallel. בֵּין בֵּית-אֵל וּבֵין הָעַי — the specific geography (between Bethel and Ai) marks the exact altar site from Genesis 12:8, completing the journey circle.
4: שֵׁם יְהוָה (šēm YHWH, the name of the LORD) — calling on the name of YHWH is the worship formula introduced in Genesis 4:26 (Enosh's generation "began to call on the name of the LORD") and repeated at each of Abram's altar sites (Genesis 12:8; here; Genesis 21:33). The act of calling on the name is covenantal and public — it marks a site as belonging to YHWH's sphere. Abram returns to the Bethel altar after the Egypt episode: the lapse of Genesis 12 is not the end of the story; the worship resumes. The Hebrew idiom qārāʾ bĕšēm YHWH includes the idea of proclaiming the name — invoking YHWH publicly over the landscape.
5: גַּם-לְלוֹט (gam-lĕlôṭ, also to Lot) — Lot is syntactically included within Abram's orbit: "also to Lot, who was going with Abram." Hahōlēḵ ʾet-ʾaḇrām (the one going with Abram) — the Qal participle presents Lot's accompanying as continuous. Lot has flocks, herds, and tents of his own — wealth accumulated during the Egypt sojourn (sharing Abram's blessing by proximity). The abundance of both men is what creates the conflict in Genesis 13:6.
6: לֹא-נָשָׂא אֹתָם הָאָרֶץ (lōʾ-nāśāʾ ʾōtām hāʾāreṣ) — "the land was not able to support them." Nāśāʾ (to carry, bear, lift) — the land literally "could not carry/bear them together." The same idiom appears in 36:7 for Esau and Jacob. The conflict of abundance is a land-carrying issue — the pastoral economy of the ancient Near East required vast territory for grazing herds; the land's limit forces the partition that the next verses describe.
7: וַיְהִי-רִיב (wayhî-rîḇ, and there was strife) — rîḇ is the legal/adversarial dispute word: a case, a lawsuit, a controversy (cf. Micah 6:1: "the LORD has a rîḇ with his people"). Even though here it is between herdsmen, the term elevates a pastoral dispute to a quasi-legal conflict. הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי אָז יֹשֵׁב בָּאָרֶץ — the parenthetical note about Canaanites and Perizzites in the land reminds the reader that Abram and Lot are resident aliens (gērîm) — their conflict risks dishonoring YHWH's name before the eyes of the land's actual occupants.
8: אַל-נָא תְהִי מְרִיבָה (ʾal-nāʾ tĕhî mĕrîḇāh) — "let there not be strife." ʾAl (negative jussive) + nāʾ (please, I pray) — gentle entreaty rather than command; Abram defers to his nephew out of love rather than asserting the senior's claim. Mĕrîḇāh (strife, contention) — the same word as the water-strife site in the wilderness (Exodus 17:7; Numbers 20:13). כִּי-אֲנָשִׁים אַחִים אֲנָחְנוּ — "for we are brothers/kinsmen." ʾAḥîm means kinsmen broadly (uncle-nephew, cousins — not just sibling brothers). The kinship claim is the moral ground for peace: family should not fight.
9: הֲלֹא כָל-הָאָרֶץ לְפָנֶיךָ (hălōʾ ḵol-hāʾāreṣ lĕpānêḵā) — "Is not the whole land before you?" The rhetorical question gives Lot the choice — Abram, the elder, cedes his priority right. אִם-הַשְּׂמֹאל וְאֵימִנָה (if left, I will go right; if right, I will go left) — the ABBA chiastic structure of the direction-offer encodes Abram's complete openness. The generosity is remarkable: Abram, the covenant recipient, gives up the right of first selection to preserve family peace. This deference contrasts with the grasping mentality of Babel and Lot's subsequent choice.
10:
כִּכַּר הַיַּרְדֵּן כִּי כֻלָּהּ מַשְׁקֶה (kikkar hayyardēn kî ḵullāh mašqeh) — "the whole plain of the Jordan, for all of it was well-watered." Kikkar (the round/circular area, the plain) — the Jordan Valley. Mašqeh (well-watered, irrigated) from šāqāh (to water, give to drink).

כְּגַן-יְהוָה כְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם — "like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt." Two comparisons: Eden (the garden of the LORD, cf. Genesis 2:8-10) and Egypt (the Nile irrigation, Genesis 12:10). Both carry ominous resonance: the garden is where humanity was expelled from; Egypt is where Abram nearly lost Sarai. The most desirable territory in Lot's eyes is associated with exile and the foreign. The narrator's proleptic "before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah" signals the fatal irony: Lot is choosing what looks like paradise, which will become destruction.
11: וַיִּסַּע לוֹט מִקֶּדֶם (wayyissaʿ lôṭ miqqeḏem) — "Lot set out eastward." Qeḏem (east, the direction of the rising sun) carries the narrative weight of departure throughout Genesis: the expulsion from Eden is eastward (Genesis 3:24); Cain goes east (Genesis 4:16); Babel is in the east (Genesis 11:2). Lot's eastward movement falls into this pattern — east marks movement away from the presence of God and toward the region of judgment. The text's structural use of qeḏem is not coincidental.
12: אַבְרָם יָשַׁב בְּאֶרֶץ-כְּנָעַן וְלוֹט יָשַׁב בְּעָרֵי הַכִּכָּר — the two men's destinations are contrasted in a single verse: Abram in the land of Canaan (the promise); Lot among the cities of the plain. וַיֶּאֱהַל עַד-סְדֹם — "and pitched his tents as far as Sodom." ʾĀhal (to tent, to pitch a tent) — Lot gradually approaches Sodom; in 14:12 he is "living in Sodom"; in 19:1 he is "sitting at the gate of Sodom" (a position of civic leadership). The progressive enculturation into Sodom happens in stages, tracked by these verbal notices.
13: וְאַנְשֵׁי סְדֹם רָעִים וְחַטָּאִים לַיהוָה מְאֹד — "now the men of Sodom were wicked and sinners against the LORD exceedingly." Two near-synonyms pile up: rāʿîm (wicked, evil — the general moral category) and ḥaṭṭāʾîm (sinners — those who miss the mark, ḥāṭāʾ). The intensifier mĕʾōḏ (greatly, exceedingly) at the end is emphatic. The phrase laYHWH (against the LORD) frames their wickedness as transgression against the covenant God specifically, not merely against social norms. This verse sets the stage for the divine inspection and judgment of Genesis 18-19.
14: שָׂא עֵינֶיךָ וּרְאֵה מִן-הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה שָׁם — "lift up your eyes and look from the place where you are." The command śāʾ ʿênêḵā (lift your eyes) is the standard prelude to a divinely directed seeing-event throughout the patriarchal narratives (Genesis 13:14; Genesis 18:2; Genesis 22:4, 13; Genesis 24:63; Genesis 31:10, 12; Genesis 33:1). Immediately after Lot departs, YHWH speaks to Abram — the covenant promise restated. The land promise is now given not merely for offspring (Genesis 12:7) but directly to Abram's sight: "all the land that you see I will give to you." The land is visualized before it is possessed.
15: כִּי אֶת-כָּל-הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה רֹאֶה לְךָ אֶתְּנֶנָּה וּלְזַרְעֲךָ עַד-עוֹלָם — "for all the land that you see, to you I will give it and to your offspring forever." ʿAḏ-ʿôlām (forever, unto eternity) — this is the perpetual/eternal form of the land covenant: the promised possession is not temporary. ʿÔlām (long duration, age, perpetuity) — when applied to the covenant (Genesis 17:7, 8, 13, 19; Exodus 31:16-17), it carries the force of an irrevocable divine commitment that transcends any single generation. Remarkably, this unconditional promise comes immediately after Lot has chosen the best land — YHWH gives Abram what Lot visually appropriated.
16: וְשַׂמְתִּי אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ — "I will make your offspring like the dust of the earth." The dust metaphor for innumerability (here) complements the star metaphor of 15:5 and the sand metaphor of Genesis 22:17. Each image emphasizes a different dimension: dust = ubiquitous, earth-covering (geographic spread); stars = sublime, heaven-reaching (transcendence); sand = borderline, coastal (the seashore of promise). ʿĀpār hāʾāreṣ (dust of the earth) echoes Adam's creation from the dust (Genesis 2:7) and Adam's return to dust (Genesis 3:19) — the offspring who will be as dust are those formed from the dust, a subtle human-solidarity image.
17: קוּם הִתְהַלֵּךְ בָּאָרֶץ לְאָרְכָּהּ וּלְרָחְבָּהּ — "arise, walk through the land in its length and in its breadth." Hitchallēḵ (Hithpael of hālak, walk about through) — the Hithpael denotes reciprocal or intensive walking, traversing completely. This same form appears in Genesis 17:1 (God's command to "walk before me") and Genesis 5:22, 24 (Enoch "walked with God"). Walking through the land is a symbolic act of possession — traversing the territory claims it (cf. Joshua 1:3: "every place your foot treads I will give to you"). The command anticipates Joshua's reconnaissance of the land; Abram is to survey what is promised.
18: אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא אֲשֶׁר בְּחֶבְרוֹן — "the oaks of Mamre which are at Hebron." ʾĒlônê mamrēʾ (oaks/terebinths of Mamre) — the great trees site at Hebron; Mamre is an Amorite chieftain allied with Abram (Genesis 14:13, 24). Abram settles at the site of his Amorite ally, near sacred trees. Ḥeḇrôn (Hebron) — the name may be connected to the root ḥāḇar (to join, to be allied/confederate) — "fellowship" — apt for a place where Abram lives among allied Amorites. Hebron will be the site of Sarah's burial (Genesis 23:19), Abram's burial (Genesis 25:9), and Israel's first royal capital under David (2 Samuel 2:3-4). מִזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה — another altar, the fourth in Abram's worship-marking of the land (Genesis 12:7, 8; Genesis 13:4; here).
